For complete answers to questions 2–8, you would need to consult the full permissions documentation or the [llms.txt index](https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt) referenced at the top.

aiTitle: Analyze Claude configuration and permissions issues

input.content:
# Permissions Friction Audit — `~/.claude/` and `~/Work/*`

**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Scope:** global `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.claude/hooks.json`, 11 hook scripts under `~/.claude/bin/`, 46 project-level settings files (3× `settings.json` + 43× `settings.local.json`) across 36 projects + worktrees, and a 218-command sample mined from 19 most-recent JSONL session transcripts.
**Mode:** read-only audit, no settings touched.

---

## TL;DR

The user's complaint (“agents ask permission even for a trivial `diff` in TMPDIR”) is real and reproducible. Direct quotes from his own session logs:

> «На каждый diff делать ask permissions? Серьёзно? Агенту доступна TMPDIR и всё равно он спрашивает разрешения…»
> — `~/.claude/projects/-Users-kabrosimov-Work-oicm-grafana-dashboards-base/7de60a26-…jsonl:80`

> «И агент опять использует `grep` и спрашивает разрешения, хотя ему дозволено.»
> — same session, line 73

> «Сейчас, часто Claude Code просит permissions на `Bash(cd "$AION_AUTOPOIESEON/mlops-be/OICM-7329…" && git status && …)`. Я подозреваю, что проблема в моём CLAUDE.md.»
> — `~/.claude/projects/-Users-kabrosimov-Work-devbox-setup/d734a13a-…jsonl:2`

The user’s hypothesis (CLAUDE.md is the culprit) is **partly wrong**: CLAUDE.md does not generate the chains directly, but it nudges agents toward verbose `cd "$VAR/…" && cmd1 && cmd2` invocations and project rules pinned to env-var prefixes, which then defeat the allow-list. The dominant breaker is **command *shape*** (env-prefix, cd-prefix, chains, multi-line), not the allow-rule *syntax* (space-glob vs colon).

A 218-command sample shows:

| Command shape | Share | Match against current global allow-list? |
|---|---|---|
| Chained (`&&`/`\|`/`;`) | 67 % | Often fails on first segment match heuristic |
| `cd <abs> &&` prefixed | 15 % | Matches `Bash(cd *)` head, then re-checks tail |
| Env-var prefixed (`VAR=val cmd …`) | 3 % | **Never matches** — no rule strips env prefix |
| Bare single command | 17 % | Usually matches |
| Multi-line / heredoc | 1 % | Matcher fragments → garbage allow entries persisted |

Across 43 `settings.local.json`, hand-counted artefacts of mis-matched “Always allow”:
- **13** shell-keyword fragments stored as bash rules (`Bash(fi)`, `Bash(done)`, `Bash(then echo "Go")`, `Bash(EOF)`, …)
- **9** bare file paths stored as bash rules (`Bash(internal/fakedata/generator.go)`, `Bash(internal/kube/config.go)`, …)
- **24** literal commands ≥120 chars (single-use, will never match again)

---

## Class A — Allow-list patterns that miss real commands

> Rules exist but the harness’s matching never reaches them because the actual command shape doesn’t fit.

### Root causes

1. **Env-var prefix is never stripped.** `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy` doesn’t match `Bash(go mod *)` because the matcher sees the literal `GOSUMDB=off …` head. There’s no global rule like `Bash(GOSUMDB=* *)` or `Bash(* go mod *)`. Same for `ENV_NAME=local uv run pytest …` — the user’s most common Python test command.
2. **`cd <abs> && …` does the same thing in two ways.** `Bash(cd *)` matches the first segment of `cd /Users/.../base && grep -rn …`, but the matcher then evaluates the tail `grep -rn "from oi_telemetry|…" …`. If the tail contains regex alternation (`|`), the conservative matcher re-prompts even when `Bash(grep *)` is in the list.
3. **Missing subcommands for tools the user *thinks* are blanket-allowed.** Global has `Bash(go test *)`, `Bash(go build *)`, `Bash(go mod *)`, `Bash(go vet *)`, `Bash(go run *)`, `Bash(go get *)`, `Bash(go install *)`, `Bash(go generate *)` — but **no** `go env`, `go list`, `go doc`, `go tool *`, `go version`. Same for git: has `git status`, `git diff`, `git log`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git blame` — but **no** `git show *` (only `git show`), `git merge-base`, `git config --get` (only that exact form, not other `git config` reads), `git rev-list`, `git ls-remote`.
4. **`Bash(cmd)` vs `Bash(cmd *)` duplicates aren’t consistent.** Some commands have both (`Bash(make)` + `Bash(make *)`, `Bash(git diff)` + `Bash(git diff *)`), others only one. Single-form rules don’t match arguments and vice versa — when you only allow `Bash(date)` but agent runs `date -u`, you re-prompt.
5. **Read-only diagnostic commands missing entirely.** `diff -r`, `comm`, `column`, `paste`, `expand`, `hexdump`, `xxd`, `od`, `md5`, `shasum`, `tee`, `nproc`, `uptime` — none are in the global allow-list, even though every one is read-only / safe. The user’s `diff /tmp/a /tmp/b` complaint maps exactly here (`diff *` IS allowed globally — see #7 below for why it still asked).
6. **No allow rule for piped-to-pager output.** `<cmd> | head -20`, `<cmd> | tail -50`, `<cmd> | wc -l` is the second most common shape. Each pipe creates a multi-command sequence the matcher won’t auto-approve unless every segment has an explicit rule.
7. **Globals collide with the `/tmp`-blocking PreToolUse hook.** `~/.claude/bin/pre-tmpdir-guard` returns exit 2 on ANY command containing `/tmp/` or `/var/tmp/`. The user’s sandbox writable list is `/tmp/claude` (subset!), so `diff /tmp/foo /tmp/bar` matches `Bash(diff *)` but is then **hard-blocked by the hook**, not by permissions. The user perceives this as a permission prompt; it is actually a hook denial that the harness surfaces similarly.
8. **Sandbox `excludedCommands` removes `git` and `docker` from the auto-allow shortcut.** `settings.json:389-394` excludes `["docker", "docker-compose", "podman", "git"]` from `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed`. So even when the sandbox could safely run `git diff`, it falls through to the prompt path unless an explicit `Bash(git diff *)` rule fires. The user’s mental model is “git is allowed”; reality is “git is sandbox-excluded and depends entirely on the allow-list”.
9. **`Bash(.claude/bin/*)` rule mismatches absolute paths.** The rule is relative; agents that resolve to `~/.claude/bin/...` (absolute) won’t match. Several tool wrappers shell out to absolute paths.
10. **`Bash(fish -c 'proj wt *')` is overly literal.** Single-quote requirement breaks if the agent uses double quotes or no quotes; matcher treats them as different strings.

---

## Class B — “Always allow” → settings.local.json pollution

> Clicking “Always allow yes” persists the **literal command** rather than a useful pattern. Over time, `settings.local.json` becomes a junkyard that doesn’t actually prevent re-prompts.

### Evidence

`~/Work/node-health-monitor/.claude/settings.local.json` (132 lines), real entries verbatim:

```
"Bash(then echo \"Go\")"
"Bash(then echo \"Python project\")"
"Bash(fi)"
"Bash(done)"
"Bash(do)"
"Bash(EOF)"
"Bash(while read f)"
"Bash(do if ! grep -q \"//go:build integration\" \"$f\")"
"Bash(internal/fakedata/generator.go)"
"Bash(internal/kube/config.go)"
"Bash(internal/health/fake_data_marker.go)"
"Bash(ENV_NAME=local OICM_MULTI_CLUSTER__MULTI_CLUSTER_MODE_ENABLED=false OICM_API__BASE_URL=\"https://oicm.dev.airgap1.lab/\" OICM_KUBECONFIG=\"$HOME/Work/configurations/airgap.yaml\" OI_NODE_HEALTH_MONITOR_LOG_LEVEL=\"debug\" timeout 20 go run:*)"
```

Worktree settings (e.g. `mlops-be/OICM-7708_debug_pvc_creation/.claude/settings.local.json`) contain **14 fully literal pytest commands** with hard-coded test file paths — none will ever match a different test invocation.

### Root causes

1. **Multi-line shell scripts are tokenised, then each token offered as “Always allow”.** A `for f in ...; do if ! grep -q ...; then echo "$f"; fi; done` becomes ~10 separate prompts, the user clicks through, and 10 useless `Bash(...)` rules persist. The harness has no “generalise this rule before persisting” step.
2. **Heredocs (`<<EOF ... EOF`) split into per-line tool calls.** Documented in `node-health-monitor/settings.local.json` where `Bash(EOF)`, `Bash(internal/fakedata/...)`, `Bash(internal/health/...)` appear — these are heredoc contents stored as bash rules.
3. **Long literal commands are stored unchanged.** A 470-char `ENV_NAME=... uv run pytest tests/foo/bar.py::TestX::test_y -v` rule will never re-match because the test selector changes every run.
4. **Env-var prefixes are baked into the persisted rule.** When the user clicks “Always allow” for `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy`, the rule `Bash(GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy)` is added. Next time the agent runs `go mod tidy` (no prefix), it re-prompts.
5. **Worktree-local settings don’t inherit from `base/`.** Every new `mlops-be/MLOPS-XXXX_*` worktree starts with an empty `settings.local.json`, so all the “always allows” the user accumulated in `base/` are useless. With ~30+ worktrees per project, this re-friction is multiplicative.
6. **`Skill(...)` rules are persisted alongside Bash rules.** Files contain mixed `Skill(plan)`, `Skill(implement)`, `Skill(go-engineer)` — appears “Always allow” for skill invocations also leaks into the same allow array. Some of these skills no longer exist (renames) so the rule is dead.
7. **`WebFetch(domain:…)` rules accumulate one-off domains.** `dev.to`, `bsonspec.org`, `dario.cat`, `go.yaml.in`, etc. — single-shot reference lookups locked in as durable rules with no expiry.
8. **No deduplication or rewriter.** Same project’s `settings.local.json` contains both `Bash(make:*)` and `Bash(make build:*)` (the second is redundant). The harness never collapses redundant rules.
9. **No size guardrail.** `node-health-monitor` is at 132 lines / ~120 rules with no warning. Loading/diffing this on every prompt is non-trivial overhead.
10. **The user has built a meta-skill (`techne-fewer-permission-prompts`) to mitigate this**, but it only handles read-only Bash and MCP — not the multi-line shell-fragment problem, and it runs reactively rather than preventatively.

---

## Class C — Hook architecture amplifies friction

### Root causes

1. **`pre-tmpdir-guard` hard-blocks instead of redirecting.** It returns exit 2 on `/tmp/` paths, which the harness presents to the model as a denial that the model must react to. Result: model re-asks or rewrites. The hook’s own help text says “Use $root/tmp/ instead”, but the model often doesn’t parse that and re-issues the command, triggering another deny → user perceives the loop as “permission prompts”.
2. **`pre-tmpdir-guard` doesn’t check macOS `$TMPDIR`.** macOS `$TMPDIR` resolves to `/var/folders/…/T/`, NOT `/tmp/`. So the hook silently passes those, while the sandbox’s writable list explicitly includes both `/tmp/claude` AND `$TMPDIR` — the user’s mental model (“TMPDIR is allowed”) is correct; the symptoms come from `diff` reading FROM `/tmp/something_else` outside `/tmp/claude/`.
3. **Multiple PreToolUse hooks chained on Bash matcher.** `pre-bash-toolchain-guard` → `pre-tmpdir-guard` → `pre_bash_safety_gate.py` → `pre-bash-boundary-wrap`. Any of them can block. Each runs a fresh interpreter; cumulative latency contributes to perceived “friction”, and any one returning exit 2 looks identical to a permission denial.
4. **`PermissionRequest` hook (`permission-auto-approve`) covers a tiny whitelist.** Only auto-approves: `echo`, `printf`, `type`, `command -v`, `id`, `whoami`, `hostname`, `uname`, `arch`, `sw_vers`, `tput`, `stty`, `locale`, plus `cd <path> && <go|uv|npm|make|cargo …>`. This is narrower than the allow-list — but the hook’s very existence proves the user knew the allow-list was leaky.
5. **`cd + env-prefix combination is unhandled by the auto-approve hook.** The normaliser strips ONE `cd <path> &&` OR one env-prefix loop, then checks. `cd /repo && GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy` is stripped to `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy`, then env-prefix is stripped to `go mod tidy`, then SHOULD auto-approve. But the hook’s SAFE switch covers `go build*|go test*|go vet*|go mod*|go get*|go install*|go generate*|go run*` — it does cover `go mod*` — yet sessions still show prompts. Suspect: shell-metachar guard at the end (`*'&&'*|*'||'*|…`) rejects anything that originally contained `&&`, even after stripping. Cause: it tests `NORMALIZED`, but the `&&` was already removed. Re-check after stripping is correct — so the *actual* gap is that not all env-prefixes (e.g. `2>&1` *suffix*, `1>/tmp/x` redirects) are stripped.
6. **PostToolUse `post-edit-lint` and `post-edit-typecheck` blocking on Edit/Write.** If they fail, the user sees “tool finished with error” after every edit — not a permission prompt but contributes to the perception of “Claude won’t do anything without me clicking something”.
7. **`Stop`-hook `stop-lint-gate` can block session exit.** When linting fails, the model is re-prompted to fix before the turn closes — adds extra silent tool churn.
8. **No telemetry on hook outcomes.** No log says “hook X exited 2 on command Y” centrally. The user can’t answer “what fraction of my prompts come from hooks vs from missing allow rules?” without re-mining JSONL by hand.
9. **`pre-plan-code-guard` fires on Edit/Write/MultiEdit.** If user is in plan mode and the agent tries to edit, this denies and surfaces as a tool failure that the model interprets as needing user input.
10. **`pre-write-completion-gate` fires on Write only.** Inconsistency with Edit: Edit-vs-Write decisions matter, and the model often picks Write where Edit would have worked, hitting this gate unnecessarily.

---

## Class D — Sandbox / permission boundary mismatches

### Root causes

1. **Sandbox writable list ≠ permission allow scope.** Sandbox allows `/tmp/claude` + `/private/tmp/claude` + `$TMPDIR` + `.` + `$TMPDIR`. But the perm allow-list has `Bash(cd *)` (any dir) and no path-scoped write rules. So a command that the model thinks should be fine often fails at sandbox boundary even when perm-allowed, and vice versa.
2. **`excludedCommands: ["docker", "docker-compose", "podman", "git"]`** means these tools always run UN-sandboxed. They then ALWAYS go through full permission evaluation — no auto-approve via `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed`. Combined with under-specified `git` allow rules (Class A #3), this is the single biggest source of git-related prompts.
3. **`enableWeakerNetworkIsolation: true`** is set in global, but project-level files don’t inherit it consistently. Some project sandboxes effectively re-enable strict isolation by overriding sandbox blocks.
4. **`allowLocalBinding: true`** but no documented listener-port allow-list — model may attempt `localhost:8080` and get prompted.
5. **macOS `$TMPDIR` vs `/tmp` confusion.** Sandbox writable list lists `$TMPDIR` (which becomes `/var/folders/…/T/`) but **not** `/tmp/foo` arbitrary subpaths. Yet the `pre-tmpdir-guard` hook *adds another layer* by blocking `/tmp/` strings unconditionally. The two layers do not agree.
6. **`Read(~/.ssh/**)` deny rule duplicates the sandbox’s `read.denyOnly` list.** This is fine (defence in depth) but the user’s settings document the same rule twice without acknowledging which actually fires.
7. **No `Write(...)`/`Read(...)` allow-list at all** except `Read(//tmp/**)` in one project. The user relies entirely on `defaultMode: acceptEdits` + deny rules. Removing acceptEdits (e.g. in a code review session) would silently produce a flood of prompts.
8. **`Skill(...)`, `Task` allow rules vary across projects.** Global allows `Task`, projects redundantly re-allow `Task` and `Skill(implement)`. Newer projects use `SlashCommand(/implement)` form — these are evaluated differently and not granted by `Task`.
9. **MCP rules use `mcp__*` glob in global** but several projects re-list narrow rules (`mcp__atlassian__getJiraIssue`). The narrow rules are dead code given the glob, but they’re not removed and `~/.claude/settings.json` has `mcp__*` which already covers all MCP tools — every project-level MCP rule is redundant.

---

## Class E — CLAUDE.md and agent prompts induce friction-prone command shapes

### Root causes

1. **CLAUDE.md teaches `git -C <path>` and discourages `cd <path> && cmd`** — but worktree-aware agents still produce `cd "$AION_AUTOPOIESEON/<proj>/<branch>" && git status && git log -3` because the agent thinks `git -C` won’t pick up env-var-resolved paths the same way. This is the literal pattern in the user’s June 19 complaint.
2. **Agents wrap commands in shell-snippets for safety** (`if [ -f go.mod ]; then ...; elif [ -f pyproject.toml ]; then ...; fi`) — see node-health-monitor’s persisted garbage. CLAUDE.md never tells them “avoid multi-line scripts as Bash tool input”.
3. **Heredocs encouraged for commit messages** (`git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF' … EOF)"`) get tokenised by the harness and produce `Bash(EOF)` allow entries.
4. **Subagents have no shared permission cache.** Each `Agent` invocation runs with fresh state; if the parent had clicked-through allows, the subagent doesn’t know. Subagent re-prompts → user clicks → entry persisted in **child** scope which the parent never sees.
5. **`/techne-*` slash-commands shell out to scripts** in `~/.claude/bin/`. The script call (e.g. `~/.claude/bin/validate-config.py`) needs its own allow rule; the absolute-path form doesn’t match `Bash(.claude/bin/*)`.
6. **Worktree convention forces re-priming.** `proj wt add <branch>` creates a fresh `.claude/settings.local.json` — the user’s docs explicitly say worktrees are siblings of `base/`, but settings don’t hardlink/symlink to base.
7. **Agent definitions enumerate tool allow-lists in frontmatter** — but those frontmatter `tools:` lists are tool *classes* (`Bash`, `Read`), not per-pattern permissions. The Bash tool class being available doesn’t mean a specific Bash command is permitted.
8. **Plan/Explore subagents trigger lots of cd-into-other-dirs.** They cross worktree boundaries (`/Users/kabrosimov/.claude/projects/-Users-kabrosimov-Work/…`) which the sandbox allows-from rules treat as new write targets.

---

## Class F — Project-scope leakage and stale rules

### Root causes

1. **3 projects have committed `.claude/settings.json`** (devbox-setup, oi-observability-lib, mlops-be) — these are checked into git and shared with the team. They use the **colon form** (`Bash(uv run pytest:*)`) which mismatches the user’s globals (space form). Not broken, but stylistically inconsistent → future syntax confusion.
2. **`mlops-be/.claude/settings.json` allows commands with embedded env-var prefixes** (`Bash(ENV_NAME=local uv run pytest:*)`). This is the team’s workaround for the same env-prefix problem. It works for `ENV_NAME=local` only — any other env value re-prompts. Lock-in to a single env value.
3. **`oi-observability-lib/.claude/settings.json` and `devbox-setup/.claude/settings.json` both grant `Edit`, `Write`, `Read(**)` blanket** — fine for solo work, but they’re committed to git, so they shape *every* team member’s default. If a teammate has a stricter local policy, the project rule undercuts it.
4. **Stale `Skill(...)` references**: `Skill(plan)`, `Skill(implement)`, `Skill(review)`, `Skill(go-engineer)`, `Skill(go-style)`, `Skill(go-patterns)`, `Skill(full-cycle)` — most are the OLD short-name form, not the current `techne-*` prefix. Some skills have been renamed; rules for the old names are dead but still in the file.
5. **`WebFetch(domain:auth.oicm.my-local)` etc. — local-only domains** persisted in committed rules: useless for anyone outside that user’s environment.
6. **Each worktree’s `settings.local.json` is born empty**, then accumulates. With ~10 worktrees per project × 4 projects ≈ 40 separate allow-lists drifting independently.
7. **No purge job.** Old worktree dirs (e.g. `MLOPS-7129_oicm_migrations…`) are still around with their old settings.local files even though the branch is long-merged. Removing the worktree leaves the file behind.

---

## Pattern summary (one-page view)

| Class | Symptom user sees | Real cause | Fix layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | “I told it `git diff` was allowed but it asked again” | `Bash(git diff *)` doesn’t cover `git show`, `git branch`, `git config --get foo`, etc. | global `settings.json` |
| A | `diff /tmp/x /tmp/y` blocked | `pre-tmpdir-guard` hook hard-blocks `/tmp/` strings | hook script |
| B | Allow-list grows endlessly | “Always allow” persists literal command; matcher never auto-generalises | harness UX (can’t fix directly) + post-hoc cleanup |
| B | Worktrees re-ask the same things | `.claude/settings.local.json` is per-worktree, not inherited | tooling: sync rule from `base/` on `proj wt add` |
| C | `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy` re-asks | `permission-auto-approve` strips env-prefix but rejects on residual `&&`/`2>&1` | edit hook script |
| C | Edit succeeds but reports failure | PostToolUse lint/typecheck gates fail; surfaced as tool error | hook script |
| D | git operations always prompt | `excludedCommands` removes git from sandbox auto-allow | global `settings.json` (review excludedCommands) |
| D | sandbox writable ≠ tmp guard | Two layers disagree about `/tmp` vs `$TMPDIR` | unify guard + sandbox rules |
| E | Complex `cd "$VAR" && a && b` re-asks | Agent-generated shape; not in any allow rule | CLAUDE.md guidance + agent prompts |
| F | Old skill/domain rules persist | No cleanup job | tooling |

---

## What I did NOT touch but flagged for review

- Existing hooks-related Python scripts (`pre_bash_safety_gate.py`, `pre_write_existing_guard.py`, `stop_format.py`) have tests under `~/.claude/bin/test_*.py` — they look well-engineered. The friction is *configuration*, not *correctness*.
- `~/.claude/bin/permission-auto-approve` is a focused 100-line shell script — easy to extend safely (Class C #5 is the main gap).
- `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` correctly forbids `cd <path> && …` in the "Cross-Cutting Rules" section, yet the user’s own sessions show this exact pattern from agents — the rule is being violated. Either the rule isn’t reaching agent contexts, or agents aren’t weighting it.
- Plugin LSP servers (gopls, pyright, typescript) — out of scope, no permission relevance.

---

## Suggested next-step directions (for your review, not pre-decided)

These are options to weigh, not a plan:

1. **Quick win:** extend global `settings.json` allow-list with the missing subcommands (Class A #3, #5). Adds ~25 rules, costs nothing, kills the most frequent re-prompts.
2. **Medium win:** rewrite `pre-tmpdir-guard` to ALLOW `$TMPDIR` and `/tmp/claude` explicitly, only blocking other `/tmp/` paths (Class C #1, D #5).
3. **Medium win:** extend `permission-auto-approve` to (a) strip `2>&1`, `> /dev/null`, `| head`, `| tail`, `| wc -l` suffixes; (b) strip multiple env-prefixes; (c) auto-approve `git diff`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git log`, `git ls-files` after normalisation; (d) auto-approve common pipes of safe commands.
4. **Medium win:** prune `excludedCommands` in sandbox config (Class D #2) — at least remove `git` so `git diff` can ride `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed`.
5. **Bigger investment:** write a one-shot cleanup script that walks `~/Work/*/.claude/settings.local.json`, removes shell-keyword fragments, removes literal commands >120 chars, deduplicates, and collapses redundant rules. Could schedule it weekly via `loop` skill.
6. **Bigger investment:** patch the worktree-create hook (`~/.claude/bin/worktree-create`) to copy `base/.claude/settings.local.json` to the new worktree.
7. **Cultural:** add a CLAUDE.md rule “Never use multi-line `if/then/fi` or heredocs as a single Bash tool call — split into multiple Read/Glob/Bash calls instead.”
8. **Diagnostic:** add a PostToolUse hook for Bash that logs `(command, was_prompted)` to a CSV so you can measure friction reduction empirically after each change.

Awaiting your review.

| Command shape | Share | Match against current global allow-list? |

| Env-var prefixed (`VAR=val cmd …`) | 3 % | **Never matches** — no rule strips env prefix |
| Bare single command | 17 % | Usually matches |
| Multi-line / heredoc | 1 % | Matcher fragments → garbage allow entries persisted |

Across 43 `settings.local.json`, hand-counted artefacts of mis-matched “Always allow”:
- **13** shell-keyword fragments stored as bash rules (`Bash(fi)`, `Bash(done)`, `Bash(then echo "Go")`, `Bash(EOF)`, …)
- **9** bare file paths stored as bash rules (`Bash(internal/fakedata/generator.go)`, `Bash(internal/kube/config.go)`, …)
- **24** literal commands ≥120 chars (single-use, will never match again)

---

## Class A — Allow-list patterns that miss real commands

> Rules exist but the harness’s matching never reaches them because the actual command shape doesn’t fit.

### Root causes

1. **Env-var prefix is never stripped.** `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy` doesn’t match `Bash(go mod *)` because the matcher sees the literal `GOSUMDB=off …` head. There’s no global rule like `Bash(GOSUMDB=* *)` or `Bash(* go mod *)`. Same for `ENV_NAME=local uv run pytest …` — the user’s most common Python test command.
2. **`cd <abs> && …` does the same thing in two ways.** `Bash(cd *)` matches the first segment of `cd /Users/.../base && grep -rn …`, but the matcher then evaluates the tail `grep -rn "from oi_telemetry|…" …`. If the tail contains regex alternation (`|`), the conservative matcher re-prompts even when `Bash(grep *)` is in the list.
3. **Missing subcommands for tools the user *thinks* are blanket-allowed.** Global has `Bash(go test *)`, `Bash(go build *)`, `Bash(go mod *)`, `Bash(go vet *)`, `Bash(go run *)`, `Bash(go get *)`, `Bash(go install *)`, `Bash(go generate *)` — but **no** `go env`, `go list`, `go doc`, `go tool *`, `go version`. Same for git: has `git status`, `git diff`, `git log`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git blame` — but **no** `git show *` (only `git show`), `git merge-base`, `git config --get` (only that exact form, not other `git config` reads), `git rev-list`, `git ls-remote`.
4. **`Bash(cmd)` vs `Bash(cmd *)` duplicates aren’t consistent.** Some commands have both (`Bash(make)` + `Bash(make *)`, `Bash(git diff)` + `Bash(git diff *)`), others only one. Single-form rules don’t match arguments and vice versa — when you only allow `Bash(date)` but agent runs `date -u`, you re-prompt.
5. **Read-only diagnostic commands missing entirely.** `diff -r`, `comm`, `column`, `paste`, `expand`, `hexdump`, `xxd`, `od`, `md5`, `shasum`, `tee`, `nproc`, `uptime` — none are in the global allow-list, even though every one is read-only / safe. The user’s `diff /tmp/a /tmp/b` complaint maps exactly here (`diff *` IS allowed globally — see #7 below for why it still asked).
6. **No allow rule for piped-to-pager output.** `<cmd> | head -20`, `<cmd> | tail -50`, `<cmd> | wc -l` is the second most common shape. Each pipe creates a multi-command sequence the matcher won’t auto-approve unless every segment has an explicit rule.
7. **Globals collide with the `/tmp`-blocking PreToolUse hook.** `~/.claude/bin/pre-tmpdir-guard` returns exit 2 on ANY command containing `/tmp/` or `/var/tmp/`. The user’s sandbox writable list is `/tmp/claude` (subset!), so `diff /tmp/foo /tmp/bar` matches `Bash(diff *)` but is then **hard-blocked by the hook**, not by permissions. The user perceives this as a permission prompt; it is actually a hook denial that the harness surfaces similarly.
8. **Sandbox `excludedCommands` removes `git` and `docker` from the auto-allow shortcut.** `settings.json:389-394` excludes `["docker", "docker-compose", "podman", "git"]` from `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed`. So even when the sandbox could safely run `git diff`, it falls through to the prompt path unless an explicit `Bash(git diff *)` rule fires. The user’s mental model is “git is allowed”; reality is “git is sandbox-excluded and depends entirely on the allow-list”.
9. **`Bash(.claude/bin/*)` rule mismatches absolute paths.** The rule is relative; agents that resolve to `~/.claude/bin/...` (absolute) won’t match. Several tool wrappers shell out to absolute paths.
10. **`Bash(fish -c 'proj wt *')` is overly literal.** Single-quote requirement breaks if the agent uses double quotes or no quotes; matcher treats them as different strings.

---

## Class B — “Always allow” → settings.local.json pollution

> Clicking “Always allow yes” persists the **literal command** rather than a useful pattern. Over time, `settings.local.json` becomes a junkyard that doesn’t actually prevent re-prompts.

### Evidence

`~/Work/node-health-monitor/.claude/settings.local.json` (132 lines), real entries verbatim:

```
"Bash(then echo \"Go\")"
"Bash(then echo \"Python project\")"
"Bash(fi)"
"Bash(done)"
"Bash(do)"
"Bash(EOF)"
"Bash(while read f)"
"Bash(do if ! grep -q \"//go:build integration\" \"$f\")"
"Bash(internal/fakedata/generator.go)"
"Bash(internal/kube/config.go)"
"Bash(internal/health/fake_data_marker.go)"
"Bash(ENV_NAME=local OICM_MULTI_CLUSTER__MULTI_CLUSTER_MODE_ENABLED=false OICM_API__BASE_URL=\"https://oicm.dev.airgap1.lab/\" OICM_KUBECONFIG=\"$HOME/Work/configurations/airgap.yaml\" OI_NODE_HEALTH_MONITOR_LOG_LEVEL=\"debug\" timeout 20 go run:*)"
```

Worktree settings (e.g. `mlops-be/OICM-7708_debug_pvc_creation/.claude/settings.local.json`) contain **14 fully literal pytest commands** with hard-coded test file paths — none will ever match a different test invocation.

### Root causes

1. **Multi-line shell scripts are tokenised, then each token offered as “Always allow”.** A `for f in ...; do if ! grep -q ...; then echo "$f"; fi; done` becomes ~10 separate prompts, the user clicks through, and 10 useless `Bash(...)` rules persist. The harness has no “generalise this rule before persisting” step.
2. **Heredocs (`<<EOF ... EOF`) split into per-line tool calls.** Documented in `node-health-monitor/settings.local.json` where `Bash(EOF)`, `Bash(internal/fakedata/...)`, `Bash(internal/health/...)` appear — these are heredoc contents stored as bash rules.
3. **Long literal commands are stored unchanged.** A 470-char `ENV_NAME=... uv run pytest tests/foo/bar.py::TestX::test_y -v` rule will never re-match because the test selector changes every run.
4. **Env-var prefixes are baked into the persisted rule.** When the user clicks “Always allow” for `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy`, the rule `Bash(GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy)` is added. Next time the agent runs `go mod tidy` (no prefix), it re-prompts.
5. **Worktree-local settings don’t inherit from `base/`.** Every new `mlops-be/MLOPS-XXXX_*` worktree starts with an empty `settings.local.json`, so all the “always allows” the user accumulated in `base/` are useless. With ~30+ worktrees per project, this re-friction is multiplicative.
6. **`Skill(...)` rules are persisted alongside Bash rules.** Files contain mixed `Skill(plan)`, `Skill(implement)`, `Skill(go-engineer)` — appears “Always allow” for skill invocations also leaks into the same allow array. Some of these skills no longer exist (renames) so the rule is dead.
7. **`WebFetch(domain:…)` rules accumulate one-off domains.** `dev.to`, `bsonspec.org`, `dario.cat`, `go.yaml.in`, etc. — single-shot reference lookups locked in as durable rules with no expiry.
8. **No deduplication or rewriter.** Same project’s `settings.local.json` contains both `Bash(make:*)` and `Bash(make build:*)` (the second is redundant). The harness never collapses redundant rules.
9. **No size guardrail.** `node-health-monitor` is at 132 lines / ~120 rules with no warning. Loading/diffing this on every prompt is non-trivial overhead.
10. **The user has built a meta-skill (`techne-fewer-permission-prompts`) to mitigate this**, but it only handles read-only Bash and MCP — not the multi-line shell-fragment problem, and it runs reactively rather than preventatively.

---

## Class C — Hook architecture amplifies friction

### Root causes

1. **`pre-tmpdir-guard` hard-blocks instead of redirecting.** It returns exit 2 on `/tmp/` paths, which the harness presents to the model as a denial that the model must react to. Result: model re-asks or rewrites. The hook’s own help text says “Use $root/tmp/ instead”, but the model often doesn’t parse that and re-issues the command, triggering another deny → user perceives the loop as “permission prompts”.
2. **`pre-tmpdir-guard` doesn’t check macOS `$TMPDIR`.** macOS `$TMPDIR` resolves to `/var/folders/…/T/`, NOT `/tmp/`. So the hook silently passes those, while the sandbox’s writable list explicitly includes both `/tmp/claude` AND `$TMPDIR` — the user’s mental model (“TMPDIR is allowed”) is correct; the symptoms come from `diff` reading FROM `/tmp/something_else` outside `/tmp/claude/`.
3. **Multiple PreToolUse hooks chained on Bash matcher.** `pre-bash-toolchain-guard` → `pre-tmpdir-guard` → `pre_bash_safety_gate.py` → `pre-bash-boundary-wrap`. Any of them can block. Each runs a fresh interpreter; cumulative latency contributes to perceived “friction”, and any one returning exit 2 looks identical to a permission denial.
4. **`PermissionRequest` hook (`permission-auto-approve`) covers a tiny whitelist.** Only auto-approves: `echo`, `printf`, `type`, `command -v`, `id`, `whoami`, `hostname`, `uname`, `arch`, `sw_vers`, `tput`, `stty`, `locale`, plus `cd <path> && <go|uv|npm|make|cargo …>`. This is narrower than the allow-list — but the hook’s very existence proves the user knew the allow-list was leaky.
5. **`cd + env-prefix combination is unhandled by the auto-approve hook.** The normaliser strips ONE `cd <path> &&` OR one env-prefix loop, then checks. `cd /repo && GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy` is stripped to `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy`, then env-prefix is stripped to `go mod tidy`, then SHOULD auto-approve. But the hook’s SAFE switch covers `go build*|go test*|go vet*|go mod*|go get*|go install*|go generate*|go run*` — it does cover `go mod*` — yet sessions still show prompts. Suspect: shell-metachar guard at the end (`*'&&'*|*'||'*|…`) rejects anything that originally contained `&&`, even after stripping. Cause: it tests `NORMALIZED`, but the `&&` was already removed. Re-check after stripping is correct — so the *actual* gap is that not all env-prefixes (e.g. `2>&1` *suffix*, `1>/tmp/x` redirects) are stripped.
6. **PostToolUse `post-edit-lint` and `post-edit-typecheck` blocking on Edit/Write.** If they fail, the user sees “tool finished with error” after every edit — not a permission prompt but contributes to the perception of “Claude won’t do anything without me clicking something”.
7. **`Stop`-hook `stop-lint-gate` can block session exit.** When linting fails, the model is re-prompted to fix before the turn closes — adds extra silent tool churn.
8. **No telemetry on hook outcomes.** No log says “hook X exited 2 on command Y” centrally. The user can’t answer “what fraction of my prompts come from hooks vs from missing allow rules?” without re-mining JSONL by hand.
9. **`pre-plan-code-guard` fires on Edit/Write/MultiEdit.** If user is in plan mode and the agent tries to edit, this denies and surfaces as a tool failure that the model interprets as needing user input.
10. **`pre-write-completion-gate` fires on Write only.** Inconsistency with Edit: Edit-vs-Write decisions matter, and the model often picks Write where Edit would have worked, hitting this gate unnecessarily.

---

## Class D — Sandbox / permission boundary mismatches

### Root causes

1. **Sandbox writable list ≠ permission allow scope.** Sandbox allows `/tmp/claude` + `/private/tmp/claude` + `$TMPDIR` + `.` + `$TMPDIR`. But the perm allow-list has `Bash(cd *)` (any dir) and no path-scoped write rules. So a command that the model thinks should be fine often fails at sandbox boundary even when perm-allowed, and vice versa.
2. **`excludedCommands: ["docker", "docker-compose", "podman", "git"]`** means these tools always run UN-sandboxed. They then ALWAYS go through full permission evaluation — no auto-approve via `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed`. Combined with under-specified `git` allow rules (Class A #3), this is the single biggest source of git-related prompts.
3. **`enableWeakerNetworkIsolation: true`** is set in global, but project-level files don’t inherit it consistently. Some project sandboxes effectively re-enable strict isolation by overriding sandbox blocks.
4. **`allowLocalBinding: true`** but no documented listener-port allow-list — model may attempt `localhost:8080` and get prompted.
5. **macOS `$TMPDIR` vs `/tmp` confusion.** Sandbox writable list lists `$TMPDIR` (which becomes `/var/folders/…/T/`) but **not** `/tmp/foo` arbitrary subpaths. Yet the `pre-tmpdir-guard` hook *adds another layer* by blocking `/tmp/` strings unconditionally. The two layers do not agree.
6. **`Read(~/.ssh/**)` deny rule duplicates the sandbox’s `read.denyOnly` list.** This is fine (defence in depth) but the user’s settings document the same rule twice without acknowledging which actually fires.
7. **No `Write(...)`/`Read(...)` allow-list at all** except `Read(//tmp/**)` in one project. The user relies entirely on `defaultMode: acceptEdits` + deny rules. Removing acceptEdits (e.g. in a code review session) would silently produce a flood of prompts.
8. **`Skill(...)`, `Task` allow rules vary across projects.** Global allows `Task`, projects redundantly re-allow `Task` and `Skill(implement)`. Newer projects use `SlashCommand(/implement)` form — these are evaluated differently and not granted by `Task`.
9. **MCP rules use `mcp__*` glob in global** but several projects re-list narrow rules (`mcp__atlassian__getJiraIssue`). The narrow rules are dead code given the glob, but they’re not removed and `~/.claude/settings.json` has `mcp__*` which already covers all MCP tools — every project-level MCP rule is redundant.

---

## Class E — CLAUDE.md and agent prompts induce friction-prone command shapes

### Root causes

1. **CLAUDE.md teaches `git -C <path>` and discourages `cd <path> && cmd`** — but worktree-aware agents still produce `cd "$AION_AUTOPOIESEON/<proj>/<branch>" && git status && git log -3` because the agent thinks `git -C` won’t pick up env-var-resolved paths the same way. This is the literal pattern in the user’s June 19 complaint.
2. **Agents wrap commands in shell-snippets for safety** (`if [ -f go.mod ]; then ...; elif [ -f pyproject.toml ]; then ...; fi`) — see node-health-monitor’s persisted garbage. CLAUDE.md never tells them “avoid multi-line scripts as Bash tool input”.
3. **Heredocs encouraged for commit messages** (`git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF' … EOF)"`) get tokenised by the harness and produce `Bash(EOF)` allow entries.
4. **Subagents have no shared permission cache.** Each `Agent` invocation runs with fresh state; if the parent had clicked-through allows, the subagent doesn’t know. Subagent re-prompts → user clicks → entry persisted in **child** scope which the parent never sees.
5. **`/techne-*` slash-commands shell out to scripts** in `~/.claude/bin/`. The script call (e.g. `~/.claude/bin/validate-config.py`) needs its own allow rule; the absolute-path form doesn’t match `Bash(.claude/bin/*)`.
6. **Worktree convention forces re-priming.** `proj wt add <branch>` creates a fresh `.claude/settings.local.json` — the user’s docs explicitly say worktrees are siblings of `base/`, but settings don’t hardlink/symlink to base.
7. **Agent definitions enumerate tool allow-lists in frontmatter** — but those frontmatter `tools:` lists are tool *classes* (`Bash`, `Read`), not per-pattern permissions. The Bash tool class being available doesn’t mean a specific Bash command is permitted.
8. **Plan/Explore subagents trigger lots of cd-into-other-dirs.** They cross worktree boundaries (`/Users/kabrosimov/.claude/projects/-Users-kabrosimov-Work/…`) which the sandbox allows-from rules treat as new write targets.

---

## Class F — Project-scope leakage and stale rules

### Root causes

1. **3 projects have committed `.claude/settings.json`** (devbox-setup, oi-observability-lib, mlops-be) — these are checked into git and shared with the team. They use the **colon form** (`Bash(uv run pytest:*)`) which mismatches the user’s globals (space form). Not broken, but stylistically inconsistent → future syntax confusion.
2. **`mlops-be/.claude/settings.json` allows commands with embedded env-var prefixes** (`Bash(ENV_NAME=local uv run pytest:*)`). This is the team’s workaround for the same env-prefix problem. It works for `ENV_NAME=local` only — any other env value re-prompts. Lock-in to a single env value.
3. **`oi-observability-lib/.claude/settings.json` and `devbox-setup/.claude/settings.json` both grant `Edit`, `Write`, `Read(**)` blanket** — fine for solo work, but they’re committed to git, so they shape *every* team member’s default. If a teammate has a stricter local policy, the project rule undercuts it.
4. **Stale `Skill(...)` references**: `Skill(plan)`, `Skill(implement)`, `Skill(review)`, `Skill(go-engineer)`, `Skill(go-style)`, `Skill(go-patterns)`, `Skill(full-cycle)` — most are the OLD short-name form, not the current `techne-*` prefix. Some skills have been renamed; rules for the old names are dead but still in the file.
5. **`WebFetch(domain:auth.oicm.my-local)` etc. — local-only domains** persisted in committed rules: useless for anyone outside that user’s environment.
6. **Each worktree’s `settings.local.json` is born empty**, then accumulates. With ~10 worktrees per project × 4 projects ≈ 40 separate allow-lists drifting independently.
7. **No purge job.** Old worktree dirs (e.g. `MLOPS-7129_oicm_migrations…`) are still around with their old settings.local files even though the branch is long-merged. Removing the worktree leaves the file behind.

---

## Pattern summary (one-page view)

| Class | Symptom user sees | Real cause | Fix layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | “I told it `git diff` was allowed but it asked again” | `Bash(git diff *)` doesn’t cover `git show`, `git branch`, `git config --get foo`, etc. | global `settings.json` |
| A | `diff /tmp/x /tmp/y` blocked | `pre-tmpdir-guard` hook hard-blocks `/tmp/` strings | hook script |
| B | Allow-list grows endlessly | “Always allow” persists literal command; matcher never auto-generalises | harness UX (can’t fix directly) + post-hoc cleanup |
| B | Worktrees re-ask the same things | `.claude/settings.local.json` is per-worktree, not inherited | tooling: sync rule from `base/` on `proj wt add` |
| C | `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy` re-asks | `permission-auto-approve` strips env-prefix but rejects on residual `&&`/`2>&1` | edit hook script |
| C | Edit succeeds but reports failure | PostToolUse lint/typecheck gates fail; surfaced as tool error | hook script |
| D | git operations always prompt | `excludedCommands` removes git from sandbox auto-allow | global `settings.json` (review excludedCommands) |
| D | sandbox writable ≠ tmp guard | Two layers disagree about `/tmp` vs `$TMPDIR` | unify guard + sandbox rules |
| E | Complex `cd "$VAR" && a && b` re-asks | Agent-generated shape; not in any allow rule | CLAUDE.md guidance + agent prompts |
| F | Old skill/domain rules persist | No cleanup job | tooling |

---

## What I did NOT touch but flagged for review

- Existing hooks-related Python scripts (`pre_bash_safety_gate.py`, `pre_write_existing_guard.py`, `stop_format.py`) have tests under `~/.claude/bin/test_*.py` — they look well-engineered. The friction is *configuration*, not *correctness*.
- `~/.claude/bin/permission-auto-approve` is a focused 100-line shell script — easy to extend safely (Class C #5 is the main gap).
- `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` correctly forbids `cd <path> && …` in the "Cross-Cutting Rules" section, yet the user’s own sessions show this exact pattern from agents — the rule is being violated. Either the rule isn’t reaching agent contexts, or agents aren’t weighting it.
- Plugin LSP servers (gopls, pyright, typescript) — out of scope, no permission relevance.

---

## Suggested next-step directions (for your review, not pre-decided)

These are options to weigh, not a plan:

1. **Quick win:** extend global `settings.json` allow-list with the missing subcommands (Class A #3, #5). Adds ~25 rules, costs nothing, kills the most frequent re-prompts.
2. **Medium win:** rewrite `pre-tmpdir-guard` to ALLOW `$TMPDIR` and `/tmp/claude` explicitly, only blocking other `/tmp/` paths (Class C #1, D #5).
3. **Medium win:** extend `permission-auto-approve` to (a) strip `2>&1`, `> /dev/null`, `| head`, `| tail`, `| wc -l` suffixes; (b) strip multiple env-prefixes; (c) auto-approve `git diff`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git log`, `git ls-files` after normalisation; (d) auto-approve common pipes of safe commands.
4. **Medium win:** prune `excludedCommands` in sandbox config (Class D #2) — at least remove `git` so `git diff` can ride `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed`.
5. **Bigger investment:** write a one-shot cleanup script that walks `~/Work/*/.claude/settings.local.json`, removes shell-keyword fragments, removes literal commands >120 chars, deduplicates, and collapses redundant rules. Could schedule it weekly via `loop` skill.
6. **Bigger investment:** patch the worktree-create hook (`~/.claude/bin/worktree-create`) to copy `base/.claude/settings.local.json` to the new worktree.
7. **Cultural:** add a CLAUDE.md rule “Never use multi-line `if/then/fi` or heredocs as a single Bash tool call — split into multiple Read/Glob/Bash calls instead.”
8. **Diagnostic:** add a PostToolUse hook for Bash that logs `(command, was_prompted)` to a CSV so you can measure friction reduction empirically after each change.

Awaiting your review.

content.text:
Отчёт готов: `~/.claude/plans/2026-06-23-permissions-audit.md`.

**Ключевые находки:**

1. **Гипотеза пользователя про CLAUDE.md — частично неверна.** Главный виновник — не синтаксис правил (space-glob vs colon — оба работают), а **форма команд**: 67 % реальных bash-вызовов это chained-команды, ещё 15 % — `cd <abs> && …`, 3 % — env-prefix вроде `GOSUMDB=off go mod tidy`. Allow-list просто не покрывает эти формы.

2. **`pre-tmpdir-guard` хук жёстко блокирует любое упоминание `/tmp/`** — отсюда твоё «diff в TMPDIR просит разрешение». На macOS `$TMPDIR` → `/var/folders/...`, не `/tmp/`, но хук фильтрует строку «/tmp/» в команде независимо от того, где она используется.

3. **`settings.local.json` — помойка.** В `node-health-monitor`: 132 строки, включая `Bash(fi)`, `Bash(done)`, `Bash(EOF)`, `Bash(internal/fakedata/generator.go)` — это фрагменты multi-line shell-скриптов, которые харнес токенизирует и сохраняет как отдельные правила, когда юзер кликает «Always allow».

4. **Worktrees не наследуют settings.local от base/** — каждый из ~40 worktrees начинает с пустым allow-list, отсюда мультипликатор re-prompts.

5. **6 классов проблем, 5-10 root causes на каждый**, плюс таблица «симптом → причина → слой фикса» и 8 направлений возможных действий (от «расширить allow-list» до «переписать worktree-create hook»).

Прямые цитаты из твоих же session logs включены — найди раздел TL;DR. Жду твоего ревью документа, потом обсудим куда двигаться.

aiTitle: Analyze Claude configuration and permissions issues

input.prompt:
You are mining Claude Code session JSONL transcripts to extract verbatim evidence for a permissions audit report. Read-only. The user wants COMPREHENSIVE data — include raw quotes, not summaries.

Session logs are at `/Users/kabrosimov/.claude/projects/*/{uuid}.jsonl`. Each line is one JSON record.

I need you to produce SEVEN output files, all under `/Users/kabrosimov/Work/devbox-setup/roles/devbox/files/dot_claude/future_projects/comprehensive_analysis/data/`. Use the Write tool to create them.

## File 1: `session-excerpts/user-complaints-verbatim.md`
Mine the 25 most recent JSONL files. Find every user message (where `.type == "user"` and `.message.role == "user"` and the content text matches `permission|разреш|prompt|спрашивает|ask again|always allow|ask|просит|кликаю|кликать|задолбал|раздражает|надоел`). For each match, write a markdown block:

```
### {session-dir-shortname} — line {N}, uuid {short-uuid}, timestamp {iso}
{full verbatim message content, no truncation}
```

Aim for 15-30 quotes. Don't paraphrase. Keep Russian text in Russian.

## File 2: `session-excerpts/agent-acknowledgements.md`
From the same mined sessions, find ASSISTANT messages (`.type == "assistant"`) that contain text acknowledging permission/rule problems — search for: `allow rule|allowlist|allow-list|allowlist|permission|разрешен|правил|hook|sandbox|excludedCommands|cd .*&&|env-prefix|GOSUMDB`. Pick 10-15 most informative ones. Same format. These show the model's own understanding of the problem at the time.

## File 3: `bash-command-samples/all-commands-classified.csv`
CSV with columns: `session,timestamp,shape,command`. Mine 100+ bash tool_use commands (from `.message.content[].input.command` where `.message.content[].name == "Bash"`). Classify `shape` as one of: `bare | cd-prefix | env-prefix | chained | multiline | piped | redirected | heredoc`. Don't truncate commands. Use proper CSV quoting (commas/quotes inside fields).

## File 4: `bash-command-samples/top-friction-commands.md`
From those 100+ commands, identify and dump verbatim the 15-25 that most often hit the prompt path. Heuristics:
- Commands containing `cd "$AION_AUTOPOIESEON/...` or `cd "$HOME/...`
- Commands chained with 3+ `&&` segments
- Commands starting with env-prefix (`VAR=val ...`)
- Commands with heredocs or multi-line `for/do/done`, `if/then/fi`
- Commands invoking unusual subcommands missing from allow-list (`go env`, `go list`, `go doc`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git ls-remote`, `git config --get`, `git merge-base`, `git rev-list`)

For each, include the full command verbatim and a one-line note about why the global allow-list misses it.

## File 5: `session-excerpts/permission-related-records.jsonl`
Concatenate raw JSONL records where the line contains "permission" as a substring, capped at 200 records. One per line. No commentary. This is raw evidence for the appendix.

## File 6: `bash-command-samples/statistics.md`
Numerical breakdown from your sample of 100+ commands:
- Counts by shape
- Top 10 most-used base commands (e.g. `git`, `go`, `make`)
- Top 10 most-used (base, subcommand) pairs (e.g. `git status`, `go test`)
- Counts of commands containing specific patterns: `cd "$`, `&&`, `2>&1`, `| head`, `| tail`, `| wc`, `>/dev/null`, env-prefix
- Count of commands that would NOT match any rule in the provided global allow-list (simulated)

## File 7: `bash-command-samples/normalization-trace.md`
Pick 8-10 representative commands that get prompted. For each, trace step-by-step:
1. Original command
2. What sandbox would do (allowed/denied/excluded)
3. What `pre-tmpdir-guard` would do (block on /tmp/?)
4. What `pre-bash-toolchain-guard` would do (block on bare pytest/go fmt?)
5. What `permission-auto-approve` would do (strip cd, strip env, check SAFE list)
6. Whether it matches any rule in the global allow-list (cite the rule if yes)
7. Final outcome: auto-allowed / blocked / prompted

Reference the user's global allow patterns from `~/.claude/settings.json` and hook scripts from `~/.claude/bin/permission-auto-approve`, `pre-tmpdir-guard`, `pre-bash-toolchain-guard`.

Constraints:
- Use jq aggressively, never load whole JSONL into context.
- Write files with the Write tool — don't dump them in your response.
- After writing all 7 files, return a SHORT summary (under 200 words): file paths, total commands sampled, total complaints found, top 3 surprises.

attachment.content:
- ui-design: UI/UX design knowledge for component specification, design tokens (W3C format), responsive layout, accessibility, and integration with Figma MCP and Storybook MCP. Use when creating design specifications, defining design tokens, planning responsive layouts, or ensuring accessibility compliance (WCAG).
- lint-discipline: Lint discipline rules — agents must fix lint issues, never suppress them with directives like noqa, nolint, or eslint-disable.
- lsp-navigation: Enforces LSP-first code navigation. Grep discovers files and text. LSP understands code. This skill ensures agents use the right tool for the job.
- python-testing: Python testing patterns with pytest, fixtures, mocking, and parametrised tests. Use when writing or reviewing Python tests, creating fixtures, setting up mocks, or structuring parametrised test cases.
- ast-grep: Structural AST code search and rewriting using ast-grep (Tree-sitter). Use instead of grep for language-aware pattern matching ("find all X that lack Y"). Use for structural search, code audits, or pattern-based refactoring.
- iterative-retrieval: Progressive context refinement pattern for subagents and research tasks. Solves the "don't know what I need" problem through DISPATCH/EVALUATE/REFINE/LOOP. Use when spawning Explore agents, when agents need to find relevant code, or when initial searches return too much noise or too little signal.
- frontend-engineer: Write clean, typed, production-ready frontend code with TypeScript, React, and Next.js. Use when implementing React components, custom hooks, pages, forms, state management, styling, or accessibility — enforces project conventions.
- frontend-testing: Frontend testing with React Testing Library, Vitest, user-event, and MSW. Use when writing frontend tests, mocking API calls, testing React components or hooks, choosing query strategies (getByRole vs getByTestId), or deciding unit vs e2e.
- python-engineer: Write clean, typed, production-ready Python code with full type hints and modern idioms. Use when implementing Python functions, services, classes, decorators, async code, data models, CLI tools, or API endpoints — enforces project conventions.
- agent-base-protocol: Shared foundational protocol for all agents. Covers file operations, language standard, and clarification rules. Referenced by all agent definitions to avoid duplication.
- config: Configurable paths, task context variables, and project directory structure for agents and commands. Single source of truth for PLANS_DIR, JIRA_ISSUE, BRANCH_NAME, PROJECT_DIR. Use when resolving project paths, looking up configuration variables, or understanding the agent system directory structure.
- techne-fewer-permission-prompts: Reduce Claude Code permission prompts by mining recent transcripts for read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then merging a prioritised allowlist into the project `.claude/settings.json`. Use this fork in place of the bundled `fewer-permission-prompts` skill — it offloads JSONL parsing and command-head extraction to a deterministic Python script (sudo/env-prefix/wrapper-leader stripping, quote-aware pipe splitting), keeping classification and merge judgement in the prompt. Also use when the user mentions allowlists, permission prompts, settings.json permissions, or wants to cut interruption from approval dialogs.
- skill-builder: Reference templates, frontmatter schema, progressive disclosure patterns, and validation checklists for building skill modules in the Claude Code agent system. Use when creating a new skill, validating skill structure, or improving skill descriptions. Also use when working with SKILL.md files, skill frontmatter, or knowledge modules.
- go-review-checklist: Go code review checklist, verification checkpoints, and review patterns. Used by the code-reviewer agent when reviewing Go files. Covers enumeration, verification, counter-evidence, backward compatibility, and domain compliance checks. Use when reviewing Go code or running verification checkpoints.
- agent-builder: Reference templates, frontmatter schema, archetype patterns, and validation checklists for building agent definitions in the Claude Code agent system. Use when creating a new agent, validating agent structure, or improving agent definitions. Also use when working with agent frontmatter, archetypes, or agent pipeline integration.
- code-writing-protocols: Shared protocols for all code-writing agents (software engineers, test writers). Covers approval validation, decision classification with full Tier 3 exploration, anti-satisficing rules, anti-helpfulness protocol, routine task mode, pre-implementation verification, comment audit self-review, and anti-laziness protocol.
- mcp-playwright: Playwright MCP patterns for browser automation, accessibility inspection, and smoke testing. Covers navigation, interaction, screenshots, accessibility tree, and console monitoring. Use when automating browser interactions, running smoke tests, taking screenshots, or inspecting accessibility.
- shared-utils: Shared utility scripts for all agents. Use when needing Jira context from branch, extracting issue keys, or using other cross-cutting utilities.
- python-tooling: Python project tooling with uv, poetry, pip. Use when discussing project setup, package management, pyproject.toml, virtual environments, or development tools. Also use when scaffolding new Python projects or configuring package managers.
- go-testing: Go testing patterns with testify suites, table-driven tests, and mocking with mockery. Use when writing or reviewing Go tests, creating test fixtures, setting up mocks, or structuring table-driven test cases.
- go-engineer: Write idiomatic, production-ready Go code per Effective Go and Go Code Review Comments. Use when implementing Go features, services, handlers, endpoints, interfaces, error handling, concurrency, or middleware — enforces project conventions and quality standards.
- mcp-sequential-thinking: Sequential Thinking MCP patterns for structured multi-step reasoning. Covers when to use sequential thinking, thought management, branching, and summary generation. Use when decomposing complex problems, performing multi-step analysis, evaluating trade-offs, or needing structured thought chains with branching. Not to be confused with `fpf-thinking` (systems framing) or `diverge-synthesize-select` (option choice) — sequential thinking is for stepwise decomposition with revisable thoughts.
- diverge-synthesize-select: Diverge-Synthesize-Select (DSS) protocol for generating diverse solution options, synthesising the best elements, and presenting structured choices. Use when exploring options, comparing approaches, or evaluating trade-offs. Not to be confused with `fpf-thinking` (problem framing) or `mcp-sequential-thinking` (step chains) — DSS generates explicit options and presents a structured choice.
- project-toolchain: Project toolchain detection and correct command prefixes per language. Prevents failures from bare tool invocations outside virtual environments. Not to be confused with `sandbox-toolchain` (Claude Code sandbox boundaries) — `project-toolchain` covers invocation ergonomics: what to type (e.g. `uv run pytest`, `pnpm test`) so commands actually work.
- sandbox-toolchain: How Claude Code's sandbox interacts with language toolchains (Go, Python, Node). Covers writable directories, network restrictions, env var configuration, and a decision tree for diagnosing tool failures in sandbox. Not to be confused with `project-toolchain` (invocation ergonomics) — `sandbox-toolchain` covers environment boundaries: what the sandbox allows (writable dirs, network allowlist, env vars).
- hooks-architecture: Claude Code hooks system architecture and patterns. Use when configuring hooks, writing hook scripts, or debugging hook behaviour. Also use when working with PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PreCompact, or Stop lifecycle events.
- code-comments: Rules for code comments and documentation. Covers narration comments (forbidden), acceptable WHY comments, and docstring policies.
- workflow: Agent pipeline, workflow commands, and development modes for the Claude Code agent system. Covers slash commands, agent roles, language discussion policy, and key conventions. Use when determining which agent or command to use, understanding the pipeline flow, or looking up workflow conventions like model selection and agent routing.
- playwright-e2e: Playwright e2e test authoring — scoped locators, strict mode, SPA navigation, test isolation. Use when writing .spec.ts files, fixing flaky e2e tests, debugging strict mode violations, or targeting repeated elements in lists/tables.
- frontend-tooling: Frontend project tooling with Next.js, Vite, pnpm, ESLint, Prettier, and TypeScript config. Use when discussing project setup, build tools, package management, linting, formatting, or development tools. Also use when working with tsconfig, Storybook configuration, or build pipelines.
- editing-claude-config: Context-optimisation patterns for editing Claude Code configuration under `roles/devbox/files/dot_claude/` (agents, skills, commands, hooks.json, settings.json, schemas, bin, templates). Use when reading or editing any file under that path, when working inside `~/.claude/` directly, or when the conversation involves modifying Claude Code's own agent/skill/command definitions. Keeps the main conversation context lean by deferring deep reads to subagents and Grep, and disabling unused MCP servers.
- fpf-thinking: First Principles Framework (FPF) protocol for systems thinking, domain modelling, and architectural trade-offs. Use when tackling complex analysis, first-principles reasoning, project characterisation, or naming discipline. Not to be confused with `mcp-sequential-thinking` (step-by-step reasoning) or `diverge-synthesize-select` (option generation + choice) — FPF frames the problem itself.
- mcp-storybook: Storybook MCP patterns — component inventory, prop discovery, pattern matching, visual reference. Use when exploring available components, checking existing stories or props, or understanding the component library before building new UI.
- docker-validation: Docker validation patterns — Dockerfile linting with hadolint, docker-compose linting with dclint, and runtime smoke testing conventions. Use when validating Dockerfiles, docker-compose files, or running container smoke tests.
- structured-output: JSON schemas for structured agent output, hybrid output pattern, pipeline state tracking, and decision logging. Enables automated pipeline coordination and downstream agent consumption. Use when producing structured JSON output, tracking pipeline state, or logging agent decisions.
- lsp-tools: LSP code intelligence for semantic navigation, refactoring, and diagnostics — when and how to use built-in LSP tools instead of grep. Use when refactoring, renaming symbols, finding references or callers, navigating to definitions, or analysing call hierarchy.
- project-preferences: Project-specific conventions and opinionated choices. Library selections, tooling mandates, coding rules that differ from community defaults. Single source of truth — never duplicate these in other skills.
- python-monolith: Flask-OpenAPI3 monolith patterns for layered DI architecture. Covers handler-service-repository layers, dependency injection, SQLAlchemy session management, feature module structure. Use when working with Flask-OpenAPI3 projects, adding API endpoints, configuring dependency injection, or structuring feature modules in the monolith.
- agent-communication: Shared patterns for agent handoffs, escalation rules, completion formats, and user interaction. Use when agents need to communicate with each other or with users.
- add-release-service: Use when working in oi-platform-installer and the task is to add a new Helm release or service to the current bundle structure. This skill covers environment flags, config validator schema updates, the correct core or app helmfile, local render validation, manifest inspection, DaemonSet preload mapping in install/images_node_mapping.json, and dynamic registry checks in generated-environments/localcluster/extra-resources/sbom-images.txt. If the new release is a backend app built on oi-backend-service, also use the add-backend-service skill.
- bump-oicharts-versions: Use when working in oi-platform-installer and the task is to bump Helm release versions for local oicharts references to the latest published versions. This skill starts from make gather-charts, uses generated-environments/<env>/extra-resources/charts.yaml as the source of truth, updates only status=update entries in helmfile templates, and verifies the bump by rerunning gather-charts.
- add-backend-service: Use when working in oi-platform-installer and the task is to add a new backend microservice, usually into the OICM app bundle, using the oi-backend-service chart. This skill follows the current bundle architecture under templates/apps/, translates the older guide in guides/how-to-add-backend-service.md to the new paths, and requires a minimal HTTPRoute by default so the service is reachable through APISIX instead of legacy Ingress.
- kubeconfig-context-merge: Use this skill when you need to add a local kubeconfig file into the global Kubernetes config at ~/.kube/config, rename the imported context to match the kubeconfig file name, back up the existing config first, and verify that the imported context exists and can reach the cluster.
- bump-fresh-releases: Use when working in oi-platform-installer and the task is to bump fresh release tags from generated-environments/<env>/extra-resources/images.yaml produced by make gather-images. This skill first asks for the target release train in <major>.<minor> form, then updates only GitLab-mapped image tags in environments/*.yaml, templates/extra-resources/extra-images.j2, and templates/apps/oicm/values/oicm.j2, treats fallback_previous_release separately from fallback_latest, and validates the result.
- fix-dynamic-registry-ci: Use when working in oi-platform-installer and CI/CD reports raw image registries in generated-environments/<env>/extra-resources/sbom-images.txt, such as quay.io, docker.io, or registry.k8s.io. This skill traces bad images from sbom-images.txt into generated manifests, identifies the owning release and chart, inspects rendered values for the exact chart version, finds the real override path in the chart or dependency, fixes dynamic registry mapping with registry.mapping.*, and validates the result with local rendering. It also covers the case where mapping is already present in installer values but is ignored because values are misplaced, duplicated, or structurally invalid.
- techne-test: Write tests for recent changes using unit test writer agent
- techne-implement: Implement current task using software engineer agent
- techne-think: Structured systems thinking using FPF (First Principles Framework)
- techne-api-design: Design API contracts (REST/OpenAPI or Protobuf/gRPC)
- techne-build-skill: Create or validate a skill module using the Skill Builder agent, with adversarial meta-review
- techne-guide: Show available capabilities, commands, and workflow examples
- techne-domain-analysis: Run domain expert to validate requirements and challenge assumptions
- techne-verify: Run pre-PR quality gate — build, typecheck, lint, test, debug scan
- techne-audit: Run library-wide audit of agent and skill definitions (freshness, consistency, or both)
- techne-status: Show pipeline progress — milestones, gates, and agent status
- techne-schema: Design database schema using database designer agent
- techne-devcontainer: Set up a devcontainer sandbox for the current project
- techne-plan: Create implementation plan from spec or requirements
- techne-options: Generate diverse solution options using DSS (Diverge-Synthesize-Select) protocol
- techne-build-agent: Create or validate an agent definition using the Agent Builder agent, with adversarial meta-review
- techne-focus: Frame and pace a task — decompose into micro-steps, surface the single next action, brake rabbit-holes
- techne-full-cycle: Run complete development cycle with 4 milestone gates (TPM → Domain → Model → Design → Plan → API → Implement → Test → Review)
- techne-validate-config: Validate Claude Code configuration - check cross-references, skill existence, and frontmatter integrity
- techne-init-workflow: Initialise agent workflow for the current project
- techne-checkpoint: Save or restore session context across exits, compaction, and new sessions
- techne-review: Review changes using code reviewer agent
- techne-design: Create UI/UX design specification and design tokens
- techne-learn: Extract a reusable pattern from the current session into a learned skill
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content: Так, окей, давай подумаем как бы нам её до версии 13 ?
Смотри, нужна именно версия : 13.0.1+security-01

1. Тег корректен
2. Пойдем по правильному пути.
3. Issue есть - OICM-8077.

Но, пока не приступай, я дам отдельную отмашку, пока запиши это в /Users/kabrosimov/Work/oiai-work-notes/Projects/Programming/OICM-8077, перелинкуй с основной заметкой. Чтобы не забыть не потерять. Прям можно план - какой MR в какой репозиторий и тд
Правильно понимаю,  что в новой сессии будет понятно что делать дальше?

Заметил тут, что во время работы software engineer делает много запросов на permissions такого рода:
1. `Bash(grep -n "^func (builder \*PanelBuilder)" $(go env                                                               
         GOMODCACHE)/github.com/grafana/grafana-foundation-sdk/go@v0.0.12/barchart/panel_builder_gen.go 2>&1 | tail -40)`
2. `Bash(grep -n "Orientation\|Stacking\|XTickLabelRotation" $(go env                                                  
         GOMODCACHE)/github.com/grafana/grafana-foundation-sdk/go@v0.0.12/barchart/panel_builder_gen.go 2>&1…)`

Мне кажется это не ок. Давай подумаем как нам снять с меня обязанность вручную аппрувить.
go mod vendor не предлагать.
Возможно, имеет смысл gomodcache, и прочие вещи держать в gitignored `.something`, или ещё другие варианты.

Давай поразмышляем, поищем по актуальной документации как claude code, так и golang. И придумаем несколько способов сделать мне жизнь проще. Хочу чтобы такого рода вещи вообще резолвились без моего вовлечения.
